name: Ignition Prereqs
desc: Environment setup, tracebacks, and the math primers ML assumes silently.

questions.card:
What: A virtual environment (venv) is a private, throwaway copy of Python and its installed packages, kept inside your project folder instead of shared system-wide.

Why: Two projects almost never need the exact same package versions — installing everything globally means the second project you set up can silently break the first. A venv per project means rm -rf .venv undoes every install with zero risk to anything else on the machine.

How: Open a real terminal, make a folder for this project, move into it, then create the venv inside it — .venv becomes a subfolder holding a private Python install.

mkdir myproject cd myproject python -m venv .venv # .venv/ now exists inside myproject/

What: Activating puts the venv's own python/pip at the front of your shell's PATH, so those commands resolve to the venv's copies instead of the system-wide ones.

Why: Without activating, pip install installs into the SYSTEM Python (or refuses, with a permission error) — completely defeating the point of having created an isolated .venv in the first place.

How: Run this in the terminal, from the same folder where .venv lives. Your prompt changes to show the venv name — that's your confirmation it's active.

source .venv/bin/activate (.venv) $ which python # .../myproject/.venv/bin/python

What: pip is Python's package installer — it downloads a library from PyPI (the Python Package Index) and adds it to whichever Python environment is currently active.

Why: Nobody hand-writes numeric arrays or HTTP clients from scratch — the entire Python ecosystem (NumPy, pandas, requests, everything) is distributed and installed this exact way, one pip install at a time.

How: With the venv activated, pip install <name> fetches the package and installs it ONLY into .venv — the system Python is untouched.

(.venv) $ pip install numpy # Successfully installed numpy-...

What: pip freeze lists every installed package with its EXACT version; redirecting it into requirements.txt saves that list to a file.

Why: "Works on my machine" is usually a version mismatch — a teammate (or your future self, or a deploy server) running pip install -r requirements.txt reproduces your exact environment instead of guessing at versions.

How: Redirect pip freeze's output into a file with >; commit that file alongside your code.

(.venv) $ pip freeze > requirements.txt # elsewhere: pip install -r requirements.txt

What: Running python file.py hands the file to the Python interpreter, which executes it top to bottom.

Why: It's the most basic feedback loop there is: write code, run it, see what happens — every larger workflow (tests, servers, notebooks) is built on top of this same single command.

How: From the terminal, with the venv active (so it's using the right interpreter and packages), name the file after python.

(.venv) $ python main.py # runs main.py top to bottom

What: A shebang is a special first line (#!...) telling the OS which interpreter should run the rest of the file, so the file can be executed directly instead of via python file.py.

Why: It's what makes a script feel like a real standalone program — CLI tools you install (black, pip itself) are plain Python files that work as bare commands specifically because of this line.

How: #!/usr/bin/env python3 asks the OS to look up python3 on PATH (portable across machines) rather than hardcoding an absolute path. It must be the very first line, and the file needs execute permission.

#!/usr/bin/env python3 print("hi") # then: chmod +x file.py && ./file.py

What: A traceback is the block Python prints when a program crashes — it's the full call chain that led to the error, ending with the exception type and message.

Why: New Python devs often panic at the wall of text and miss that the answer is always on the LAST line — reading tracebacks calmly and bottom-up is the single highest-leverage debugging skill there is.

How: Ignore the middle (the call stack, useful later for locating WHERE); the last line has the shape ExceptionType: message — that's the error's name and why it happened.

Traceback (most recent call last): File "main.py", line 3, in <module> 1 / 0 ZeroDivisionError: division by zero # <- read this last line first
questions.mission: Assign the error name into err.

questions.card:
What: if __name__ == "__main__": guards code so it only runs when the file is executed directly — not when it's imported as a module by another file.

Why: Without it, importing your file to reuse a function elsewhere would ALSO re-run every top-level statement (print output, script logic) as a side effect — the guard is what lets a file be both a runnable script AND a safely importable module.

How: Python sets the special variable __name__ to "__main__" only when a file is run directly (never when imported) — so code inside this if is the "only when run directly" section.

def main(): print("running") if __name__ == "__main__": main()

What: uv is a modern, much faster drop-in replacement for the pip + venv combo, written in Rust.

Why: The classic workflow (python -m venv, then pip install one at a time) is reliable but slow — uv does the same job in a fraction of the time and is fast becoming the default choice for new projects.

How: uv add <package> creates the venv if needed AND installs the package, tracking it in a project file automatically — one command instead of two.

$ uv add pandas # venv created (if missing) + pandas installed + tracked

What: A Jupyter kernel is the specific Python environment a notebook actually runs code in — registering one makes your project's venv show up as a selectable option inside Jupyter.

Why: Without registering a kernel, a notebook silently falls back to some OTHER Python (often the system one) — you install a package in your venv, then can't understand why the notebook says it's missing.

How: python -m ipykernel install, run with the venv active, registers THIS Python as a named kernel Jupyter can list and select.

(.venv) $ python -m ipykernel install --user --name proj # then in Jupyter: Kernel > Change Kernel > proj

What: The dot product of two vectors multiplies them element-by-element and sums the results into a single number: Σ aᵢbᵢ.

Why: It's the single most-repeated operation underneath machine learning — every neuron in a neural network computes a dot product between its inputs and its weights before anything else happens.

How: np.dot(a, b) does the multiply-and-sum in one compiled call instead of a Python loop over indices.

a = np.array([1, 2, 3]) b = np.array([4, 5, 6]) d = np.dot(a, b) # 1*4 + 2*5 + 3*6 = 32

What: Matrix multiplication combines two matrices (or a matrix and a vector) via @ — each output entry is a dot product of a row from the left and a column from the right.

Why: A whole layer of a neural network processing a whole batch of inputs at once is exactly one matrix multiply — it's how thousands of dot products happen in a single, GPU-friendly instruction instead of a Python loop.

How: M @ v multiplies matrix M by vector v; shapes must line up (M's column count = v's length).

M = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]]) v = np.array([5, 6]) out = M @ v # [1*5+2*6, 3*5+4*6] = [17, 39]

What: Transpose (.T) flips a matrix over its diagonal — rows become columns and columns become rows.

Why: Matrix shapes have to match exactly to multiply — transpose is the standard fix when your data is laid out sideways from what an operation expects (a very common real bug: "shapes (3,4) and (3,2) not aligned").

How: M.T returns a new view where M.T[i][j] == M[j][i] — an (m, n) matrix becomes (n, m).

M = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]) MT = M.T print(MT.shape) # (3, 2), was (2, 3)

What: The L2 norm (‖v‖) of a vector is its length — the square root of the sum of its squared components, the straight-line distance from the origin.

Why: "How big is this vector" comes up constantly: normalizing a vector to length 1, measuring how far a prediction is from the truth, and regularization terms in ML loss functions all start from this exact quantity.

How: np.linalg.norm(v) computes it directly, equivalent to sqrt(sum(v**2)).

v = np.array([3, 4]) n = np.linalg.norm(v) # sqrt(9 + 16) = 5.0

What: The inverse of a matrix M is the matrix M⁻¹ such that M @ M⁻¹ gives the identity matrix — the matrix equivalent of "1/x".

Why: It's how you solve a linear system directly (x = M⁻¹ @ b solves M @ x = b) — the same algebra behind fitting a linear regression's coefficients in closed form.

How: np.linalg.inv(M) computes it — only defined for square matrices, and only when M is non-singular (its determinant isn't zero).

M = np.array([[2, 0], [0, 4]]) inv = np.linalg.inv(M) # [[0.5, 0], [0, 0.25]]

What: The sigmoid function σ(x) = 1/(1+e⁻ˣ) squashes any real number into the open interval (0, 1), with an S-shaped curve centered at 0.5.

Why: Squashing an unbounded number into (0, 1) is exactly what's needed to interpret an output as a PROBABILITY — sigmoid is the classic activation for binary classification's final layer, for exactly this reason.

How: np.exp(-x) computes e⁻ˣ; large positive x pushes the result toward 1, large negative x toward 0.

x = 0 s = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x)) # 0.5, the midpoint

What: Softmax turns a list of raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1 — exponentiate each score, then divide by the sum of all the exponentials.

Why: It's the standard last layer for multi-class classification ("which of these 10 digits is this") — this step specifically (subtracting the max before exponentiating) is what keeps np.exp from overflowing on large scores, a real numerical-stability bug beginners hit immediately without it.

How: x - x.max() shifts every score so the largest becomes 0 before exponentiating — the final probabilities are identical, but nothing overflows.

x = np.array([1000, 1001, 1002]) e = np.exp(x - x.max()) # safe: exponents are now <= 0

What: Gradient descent is the update rule that trains almost every ML model: nudge the parameters a little in the direction that most reduces the loss, repeat.

Why: It's the mechanism underneath "training" for everything from linear regression to giant neural networks — every framework's optimizer.step() is ultimately doing this one line of math, just with extra bookkeeping.

How: grad points in the direction of STEEPEST INCREASE of the loss, so subtracting it moves downhill; eta (the learning rate) controls how big each step is.

theta = theta - eta * grad # eta too big -> overshoots; too small -> trains forever

What: Mean squared error (MSE) measures how wrong a model's predictions are: subtract, square (so errors can't cancel and big misses are punished more), then average.

Why: It's the default loss function for regression — training a model IS the process of adjusting parameters to make this exact number smaller, which is why it needs to be differentiable (squaring is; absolute value has a kink at zero).

How: (y - yhat) ** 2 gives the squared error for every example; .mean() collapses that array into one number summarizing overall performance.

y = np.array([3, 5]) yhat = np.array([2.5, 5.5]) L = ((y - yhat) ** 2).mean() # ((0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2) / 2 = 0.25

What: Bayes' rule — P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A)/P(B) — updates a belief about A after observing evidence B, in terms of quantities that are easier to know directly.

Why: It's the mathematical backbone of an entire family of ML approaches (naive Bayes classifiers, Bayesian inference, spam filters historically) — anywhere you update a probability given new evidence, this is the rule doing it.

How: lik (likelihood, P(B|A)) times prior (P(A), belief before evidence) gives the numerator; dividing by evidence (P(B)) normalizes it back into a valid probability.

lik, prior, evidence = 0.9, 0.01, 0.1 post = lik * prior / evidence # updated belief in A, given B
